summary:
The Justinianic Reconquest (533–554 AD) was the ambitious military campaign of [[event:2119|Emperor Justinian I]] to recover the western territories of the Roman Empire that had fallen to Germanic kingdoms in the previous century. Directed primarily by the brilliant general Belisarius, the reconquest achieved remarkable early successes: the Vandal Kingdom of North Africa fell in just two campaigns in 533–534 AD, and the Ostrogothic Kingdom of Italy was invaded in 535 AD. The reconquest appeared to fulfill Justinian's dream of restoring the unified Roman Empire under Byzantine rule.

The reality proved far more complicated and costly than the initial victories suggested. The Gothic War in Italy dragged on for nearly twenty years (535–554 AD), devastating the Italian peninsula through repeated battles, sieges, famines, and plague. The sophisticated urban culture of Ostrogothic Italy — which had preserved much of Roman administrative tradition under Theodoric — was systematically destroyed not by the Goths but by the war of reconquest itself. Constantinople's military overextension in the west simultaneously weakened Balkan defenses against Slavic and Avar incursions, and strained imperial finances to the breaking point. The reconquered territories in Italy and Spain were lost again within decades — Italy falling to the Lombards just three years after Justinian's death in 568 AD. Historians debate whether the reconquest ultimately strengthened or fatally weakened the Byzantine Empire, consuming the resources that might have better prepared it against the catastrophic Persian and Arab conquests of the following century.
